welcomeMessage.en: Today, we're taking a trip down memory lane back to our school days.
welcomeMessage.zh: 今天，我们将开启一段回忆之旅，回顾我们的学生时代。

welcomeMessage.en: We'll talk about our favorite subjects, funny school stories, and the real value of college education.
welcomeMessage.zh: 我们将谈论我们最喜欢的科目，学校里的趣事，以及大学教育的真正价值。

welcomeMessage.en: Get ready to share some nostalgic stories from your classroom!
welcomeMessage.zh: 准备好分享一些关于课堂的怀旧故事吧！

questionsToThinkAbout.en: What did you usually eat for lunch when you were in middle school?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 上初中的时候，你午饭通常吃什么？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: What was your major in college/university? Why did you choose it?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你在大学的专业是什么？你为什么选择它？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: Can you tell us about the most difficult exam you have ever taken?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你能给我们讲讲你参加过的最难的考试吗？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: Do you think going to university is absolutely necessary to be successful in life? Why or why not?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你认为上大学对于在生活中获得成功是绝对必要的吗？为什么？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: What games did you play during the school holidays when you were a child? Did you ever jump rope?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你小时候在学校假期都玩些什么游戏？你跳过绳吗？

vocabulary.definition: The process of receiving or giving systematic instruction, especially at a school or university.
vocabulary.example: Getting a good education is very important for your future career.

vocabulary.definition: A school for children in the grades between elementary school and high school.
vocabulary.example: When I was in middle school, I had to wear a uniform every day.

vocabulary.definition: A game or exercise in which a person jumps over a swinging rope.
vocabulary.example: During our break time, my friends and I used to jump rope on the playground.

vocabulary.definition: A formal test of a person's knowledge or proficiency in a subject or skill.
vocabulary.example: I was very nervous because the final math exam was incredibly difficult.

vocabulary.definition: Up to (the point in time or the event mentioned).
vocabulary.example: I stayed at the library reading until it closed at 10 PM.

vocabulary.definition: A student's principal subject or course of study.
vocabulary.example: When she went to university, her major was computer science.

vocabulary.definition: The process of dealing with or controlling things or people.
vocabulary.example: He is studying business management because he wants to be a boss one day.

vocabulary.definition: An establishment providing accommodations, meals, and other services for travelers and tourists.
vocabulary.example: During the holiday, we stayed at a beautiful hotel near the beach.

vocabulary.definition: An extended period of leisure and recreation, especially one spent away from home or in traveling.
vocabulary.example: Students always look forward to the long summer holiday.

vocabulary.definition: A high-level educational institution in which students study for degrees.
vocabulary.example: After high school, you can choose to go to college or start working.

vocabulary.definition: Required to be done, achieved, or present; needed; essential.
vocabulary.example: Is it really necessary to buy such an expensive laptop for school?
To be successful, you need to work hard and never give up on your dreams.

sessionSummary: Today's class took us on a trip down memory lane! We started by sharing fun stories from our childhood and middle school days. We talked about what we used to eat for lunch, playing games like jump rope with our classmates, and staying up late studying for difficult exams.
The conversation then shifted to higher education. We discussed the differences between college and university, and talked about the specific subjects we studied, such as Hotel Management.

Finally, we had a very deep and interesting debate about the future. We asked the big question: Is a traditional education absolutely necessary to be successful in life? Everyone shared great examples and different perspectives on what true success looks like!

keyVocabulary.example: "Getting a good education is very important for your future career."
"When I was in middle school, I had to wear a uniform every day."
"During our break time, my friends and I used to jump rope on the playground."
"I was very nervous because the final math exam was incredibly difficult."
"I stayed at the library reading until it closed at 10 PM."
"When she went to university, her major was computer science."
"He is studying business management because he wants to be a boss one day."
"During the holiday, we stayed at a beautiful hotel near the beach."
"Students always look forward to the long summer holiday."
"After high school, you can choose to go to college or start working."
"Is it really necessary to buy such an expensive laptop for school?"
"To be successful, you need to work hard and never give up on your dreams."

grammarTips.title: Using 'Eat [something] for lunch'
grammarTips.description: In English, when we talk about what we ate at a specific meal, we use the preposition 'FOR'. Do not use 'as' or 'to'.

grammarTips.title: Using 'I have ever...'
grammarTips.description: The phrase 'I have ever' is most commonly used with superlatives (the best, the worst, the hardest) to describe the ultimate experience in your life up to now.
grammarTips.incorrect: "This is a hard exam I have ever taken."
grammarTips.correct: "This is THE HARDEST exam I have ever taken. / That is the best hotel I have ever stayed in."

grammarTips.title: University vs. College
grammarTips.description: In the United States, 'college' and 'university' are often used to mean the exact same thing in daily conversation (higher education after high school). In the UK, they can mean slightly different things, but for general English chat, you can use either one!
grammarTips.correct: "I am going to college next year." = "I am going to university next year."

grammarTips.title: Giving Examples: 'For example'
grammarTips.description: Use 'for example' at the beginning or in the middle of a sentence to provide proof of your point. It is a great way to make your speaking sound more fluent.
grammarTips.correct: "Many jobs are hard. FOR EXAMPLE, being a doctor is very stressful."

goals: Ensure students stop using 'as' when describing meals and correctly use 'for lunch', 'for breakfast'.
Help students practice forming superlatives accompanied by 'I have ever...'.
Encourage full sentences using 'For example' when engaging in debate about success.

phases.title: Phase 1: Childhood Memories (15-20 Minutes)
phases.description: Warm-up with nostalgic stories from middle school.
questions: What did you usually eat for lunch when you were in middle school?
Did you ever jump rope or play other games during school holidays?

phases.title: Phase 2: Higher Education & Majors (20-30 Minutes)
phases.description: Discuss college life and use superlatives.
questions: What was your major in college/university? Why did you choose it?
Can you tell us about the most difficult exam you have ever taken?

phases.title: Phase 3: The Big Debate (15-20 Minutes)
phases.description: Lead a conversation about success, encouraging the use of 'for example' and 'necessary'.
questions: Do you think going to university is absolutely necessary to be successful in life? Why or why not?
